turns.content: I just finished implementing a basic reader using the seqlock crate and I'm wondering if there's a better way to handle this. My current approach works but it feels like I might be missing something about how the writer increments the sequence number.

Hey, your implementation is definitely functional, so no need to panic yet. The key thing you should keep in mind is that seqlock relies on optimistic reads where the reader checks the sequence number before taking a lock. This prevents ABA problems because even if the data changes multiple times between two checks of the same sequence value, the sequence itself will have incremented and changed.
That makes sense but I'm still confused about whether this is truly lock-free or if there are hidden costs I should be aware of when comparing it to a standard mutex approach.

It's fully lock-free since seqlock uses compare-and-swap instructions under the hood without any blocking locks. However, you do pay a small penalty in CPU cycles for checking the sequence number and validating CAS success on every read attempt compared to a simple mutex which just waits until a thread releases it.
Got it so if I'm doing millions of reads per second should I still consider using seqlock over a regular Mutex?
Yes, absolutely. For high-frequency reads where the write rate is relatively low, seqlock offers much better throughput because readers don't block when writers are updating data.
Perfect that's exactly what I needed to know so I can optimize our monitoring tool now thanks!
You're welcome! Just remember that if your writes become very frequent relative to reads, you might want to reconsider the strategy since seqlock shines when reads dominate over writes.
